text: The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection. Black-colored lymph nodes are visible due to a tattoo placed distal to the tumor. The duodenum is identified, and the dissection follows the white line laterally. The inferior vena cava and ureter are also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection for a right colectomy. Black-colored lymph nodes are visible due to a tattoo placed distal to the tumor. The duodenum is identified, and the dissection follows the white line laterally. The inferior vena cava and ureter are also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle at its junction with the superior mesenteric vessels, dissecting through the surrounding fatty tissue to expose the blue superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic artery is taken, followed by further dissection in the area.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle at its junction with the superior mesenteric vessels, dissecting through fatty tissue to expose the blue superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic artery is taken, and further dissection reveals the head of the pancreas and black-colored lymph nodes. The surgeon carefully elevates tissue while staying on top of the superior mesenteric vein, identifying the ileocolic vein.

The surgeon exposes the head of the pancreas and identifies black-stained lymph nodes. The ileocolic vein is visible as the surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, carefully navigating around the tumor adherent to the sidewall.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, encountering some challenges due to the tumor's adherence to the sidewall. With excellent exposure, the surgeon isolates the right colic vessels at their junction with the superior mesenteric vessel, and proceeds to divide the right colic vein and artery.

The surgeon isolates and divides the right colic vessels at their junction with the superior mesenteric vein, then continues dissection along the transverse colon mesentery towards the hepatic flexure. The right branch of the middle colic artery is identified and taken, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon continues dissection along the transverse colon mesentery, moving towards the hepatic flexure. A branch of the loop of Henle is visible. The right branch of the middle colic artery is isolated and taken. A sponge is placed in the dissected space to protect the duodenum and pancreas. The surgeon approaches from a super mesocolic angle and begins to split the omentum at the level of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon places a sponge in the dissected space to protect the duodenum and pancreas, approaching from a super mesocolic angle. The omentum is split at the level of the falciform ligament, and the gastrocolic omentum is disconnected distal to the gastroepiploic vessel, which is preserved. The cut edge of the gastro-mental vessel is visible to the left of the vessel sealer, indicating the completion of the medial dissection.

The surgeon disconnects the gastrocolic omentum distal to the gastroepiploic vessel, preserving the vessel while joining the medial dissection. The cut edge of the gastro-mental vessel is visible to the left of the vessel sealer. The hepatic flexure is released, and the surgeon dissects into the sidewall of the ascending colon, aiming for an R0 resection by addressing potential tumor invasion. The dissection proceeds from an inferior approach, with careful attention to the location of the ureter.

The surgeon releases the hepatic flexure and approaches the portion of the ascending colon adherent to the sidewall. Dissection is performed into the sidewall to ensure complete removal of any potential tumor invasion, aiming for an R0 resection. The surgeon carefully navigates from an inferior approach, ensuring the ureter is identified and avoided during the procedure.
The surgeon returns to the inferior approach to complete the dissection, maneuvering around the previously dissected areas to finalize the procedure.

The surgeon approaches from an inferior angle to complete the dissection, taking the remaining small bowel mesentery. The colon margin is divided using a stapler, which is inserted through the suprapubic port. Stay sutures are placed, and an isoparastaltic anastomosis is lined up. The specimen will be exteriorized through a fin and steel incision to minimize hernia risk.

The surgeon takes the remaining small bowel mesentery and checks perfusion, dividing the colon margin first. A stapler is inserted through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for specimen exteriorization via a Pfannenstiel incision. Stay sutures are placed, and an isoperistaltic anastomosis is lined up. A collotomy and enterotomy are made, and the vascular load stapler is used for the anastomosis, aided by pulling on the near side stay suture.

The surgeon creates a collotomy and an enterotomy, then inserts a vascular load stapler through the suprapubic port to perform an anastomosis. The near side stay suture is pulled to facilitate stapler insertion. After cutting the stay suture, a 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to close the common channel with a baseball stitch technique. The surgeon checks ICG perfusion and continues suturing around the corners, using an imbricating suture to secure the anastomosis.

The surgeon cuts the near side stay suture to visualize the corner, then uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to close the common channel with a baseball stitch technique. The suture is applied starting from the near side, moving out and around the corner, followed by an imbricating suture technique. The surgeon checks ICG perfusion, ensuring adequate blood flow, and continues suturing around the far corner to complete the closure.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis for the right colon, covering it with residual omentum. The specimen is extracted using a fan and seal technique, revealing its large size.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis on the right colon, covering it with residual omentum. The specimen is extracted through a fan and seal incision, revealing its large size.
